findings.message: `evaluate_error_type` never checks for undeclared enum variants, so an `ErrorType` whose code enum has extra members still returns `Blue` whenever all expected variants are present. That drops the reverse-extra half of the new §S action mapping for error types.

`evaluate_error_type` still has the old `expected_variants.is_empty()` early return, so an `ErrorType` entry with no declared variants never reaches the new reverse-extra check. If code contains undeclared enum variants, this path still reports `Blue` with no `extra_items`, which contradicts the new action-aware §S mapping.

`evaluate_error_type` short-circuits to `Blue` when `expected_variants` is empty, which bypasses the new reverse-extra check entirely. That means an `ErrorType` declared with no variants can still come back `Blue` even if the code enum contains extra variants, so the §S action-aware mapping is not applied consistently for error types.

`evaluate_secondary_adapter` still only checks the trait names declared in `implements`; it never scans the code's full `trait_impls` set for undeclared implementations. As a result, an adapter that implements an extra workspace trait can never emit the reverse-extra signal required by §S, so `modify`/`reference` cases can be misclassified as Blue/Yellow instead of Yellow/Red.

`FreeFunction` carries `module_path` specifically to disambiguate functions in different modules, but the renderer still keys both `node_id` and the lookup index only by `entry.name()`. Two valid entries like `foo::run` and `bar::run` will collapse into one node and merged edges, so the contract map becomes incorrect for same-named free functions.

The renderer coverage test is still the old 13-kind fixture and never constructs `TypeDefinitionKind::FreeFunction`, so the new public `FreeFunction` rendering path (`node_shape`/`methods_of`) can regress without this test catching it. T002 makes this a 14th kind; add a FreeFunction entry and assertion here, and rename the test accordingly.

The new public schema extension APIs are not covered by tests: `SchemaExport::with_trait_origins`/`trait_origins` and `FunctionInfo::with_module_path`/`module_path` have no assertions in the in-file test module. Existing coverage only exercises `SchemaExport::new` and unrelated module-path APIs, so a regression that drops trait origins or a function module path would pass. Add focused happy-path/default-value tests for these new fields.

`evaluate_members_check` treats reverse extras as name-only, so a field whose name matches but whose type differs is never counted as extra. For `Add` entries that underreports the reverse-extra side and can turn a spec/code type mismatch into Yellow instead of Red.

`FreeFunction` delete entries are not handled with delete semantics: the reverse pass explicitly skips `TypeAction::Delete`, and the forward result is still derived from `missing_items` only. A deleted function that is still present can therefore come back Blue instead of the required Yellow.

`FreeFunction` entries with `TypeAction::Delete` are handled incorrectly: the forward evaluator never inverts the presence check the way `evaluate_delete` does for other kinds, and the new reverse-extra pass explicitly skips `Delete`. As a result, a free function that should be considered "not yet deleted" can stay `Blue`, and a deleted-but-still-absent function can be reported `Yellow` instead of `Blue`.

`FreeFunction` delete entries skip the module-scoped reverse-extra pass entirely. That means a module with only `delete` catalogue entries can still contain undeclared functions and be reported Blue, even though `signal_for_reverse_extra` says delete should still surface reverse extras as Yellow.

`FreeFunction` lookup now scopes by `(name, module_path)`, but consistency still collapses signals and entry matching to `(type_name, kind_tag)` only. If two free functions share the same short name in different modules, their signals become indistinguishable and one module's status can mask the other in coverage/yellow/red checks.

The new FreeFunction test block never exercises `module_path=None`; every case hard-codes `Some("usecase::track")`. That leaves the top-level lookup/reverse-scope branch in `evaluate_free_function` and `evaluate_delete` unverified, even though the brief explicitly called for `None`/`Some` coverage.

`check_consistency` still only computes undeclared `types` and `traits`; it never inspects `graph.functions()` or `baseline.functions()`. After adding `TypeDefinitionKind::FreeFunction` and `TypeGraph::functions`, an undeclared public `fn` can still slip through the consistency path with no reverse signal, so the new free-function surface is only partially validated.

`check_consistency` still classifies every non-trait entry with `baseline.has_type(name)`, so `FreeFunction` declarations are never matched against `baseline.functions()`. As a result, `add`/`modify`/`reference` contradictions and `delete` validation are wrong for free functions: a function that already exists in the baseline will be treated as absent, producing false `delete_errors` and missing baseline contradictions.

`check_consistency` never iterates `baseline.functions()`, so free functions captured in `TypeBaseline::functions` are never compared the way types and traits are. As a result, a baseline free function that is deleted or structurally changed in code is silently ignored instead of producing a group-3 Red/skipped outcome, which leaves the new function baseline snapshot unused for drift detection.

`evaluate_free_function` always constructs `TypeSignal` with an empty `found_items` vector, so a matching FreeFunction never records the signature that was found. That drops evidence from the serialized signal/CLI output and makes FreeFunction results inconsistent with the other evaluators, which all populate `found_items`.

`check_type_signals` keys coverage by only `(entry.name(), kind_tag)`, but `FreeFunction` identity is `(name, module_path)`. Two declared functions with the same short name in different modules collapse to one key, so a missing or stale signal for one module can still satisfy coverage as long as another `foo/free_function` signal exists. That weakens the fail-closed gate for CN-07 module-scoped functions.

The reverse-signal conversion path still only handles undeclared types and traits. `check_consistency` now records `undeclared_functions`, but existing callers using this public helper will not get Red `TypeSignal`s for new undeclared free functions, so AC-06 group-4 drift can be collected in the report and then omitted from the signal gate.

`check_consistency` is supposed to skip unchanged baseline functions in group 3, but the FreeFunction post-pass marks every same-module function that is not declared in the current catalogue as a reverse extra before the baseline filter runs. For a track that adds `new_fn` in a module already containing unchanged baseline function `old_fn`, `old_fn` is skipped by `baseline.has_function` later, yet the forward signal for `new_fn` is already Red due to this block. The reverse-extra calculation needs to account for baseline functions or be applied after baseline filtering in the consistency path.

Typestate entries are part of the struct-based expected_members set, but evaluate_typestate never verifies that the matched TypeNode is a Struct. A public enum or type alias with the same name, no outgoing transitions, and matching/empty members can return Blue for a Typestate declaration, unlike the other struct-based evaluators that reject non-struct kinds. Add the same TypeKind::Struct guard before evaluating transitions/members.

FreeFunction signals still drop `module_path` here and are keyed only by `(type_name, kind_tag)`. That makes two `save_track` entries in different modules collide in `check_type_signals`, so the gate cannot distinguish or cover them independently.

`delete_errors` records only `name` for `FreeFunction` deletes. Because free functions are keyed by `(name, module_path)` everywhere else and same short names are valid in different modules, invalid-delete diagnostics become ambiguous and the CLI can suppress or report the wrong signal.

`check_consistency` is baseline-aware, but it calls `evaluate_type_signals` before the group-3 baseline skip logic. This FreeFunction reverse pass then marks every same-module function not declared in the current catalogue as an extra, including unchanged functions that exist in the baseline and should be skipped. A track adding `new_fn` in a module that already has unchanged baseline `old_fn` will get a Red signal for `new_fn` even though `old_fn` is correctly skipped later by `baseline.has_function`. The reverse-extra calculation needs to account for baseline functions in the consistency path.

Switching `FreeFunction` signals to fully-qualified `type_name` values (`module_path::name`) is not backward-compatible with the repo’s existing signal consumers, which still match and render signals by the catalogue entry’s short `name`. As a result, free-function rows will stop being associated with their signals unless every downstream consumer is updated in lockstep.

`check_type_signals` still keys coverage by `(type_name, kind_tag)`, while FreeFunction signals use only the short function name. Two FreeFunction entries with the same name in different modules therefore collapse to one key, so a missing or stale signal for one module can be masked and the gate can pass with incomplete coverage.

The FreeFunction coverage check only compares total counts, so a stale or wrong FreeFunction signal can satisfy coverage for a different entry. For example, one `FreeFunction` entry named `save_track` plus one Blue `free_function` signal named `delete_track` has equal counts, passes this block, has no Red/Yellow, and `check_type_signals` returns pass even though `save_track` was never evaluated. This violates the gate's "every entry must have a matching signal" rule; use at least a per-short-name multiset/count check while keeping the duplicate-module count handling.

Group-3 FreeFunction baseline comparison uses full ParamDeclaration equality, so a baseline function whose parameter binding name changes but whose parameter types/order, returns, and async flag are unchanged is reported as baseline_red_functions. The FreeFunction evaluator’s signature matcher intentionally ignores parameter names and compares only types/order, so undeclared baseline functions are judged by a stricter rule than declared FreeFunction entries and can produce false Red drift signals.

`check_type_signals` no longer has a general Red-signal gate for non-empty catalogues. As a result, any Red `TypeSignal` from `check_consistency` or `undeclared_functions_to_signals()` is ignored unless the catalogue is empty or there is also a declared Yellow entry, which contradicts the documented "Any Red signal -> error" rule and regresses the existing red-signal test.

This command reads `items_dir/<track_id>/spec.json` directly without the symlink guard used by the sibling spec-verification paths. A symlinked track directory or spec file can therefore escape the trusted tree and make the CLI hash an arbitrary file.

The new public subcommand has no tests in this change for the happy path and the failure branches (`--anchor` hit/miss, invalid track id, malformed/missing spec.json). Given the file I/O and branching here, regressions will go unguarded.

This helper reads `items_dir/<track_id>/spec.json` directly without the symlink guard used by the neighboring TDDD commands. A symlinked `items_dir`, track directory, or `spec.json` can make the CLI hash files outside the trusted tree and leak their contents via stdout.

No tests were added for the new public `execute_spec_element_hash` path, so the success case and the failure paths introduced here (`missing spec.json`, schema failure, missing anchor) are not covered.

The new tests only assert that `execute_spec_element_hash` succeeds or fails; they never verify stdout. That leaves the core behavior of this command unprotected, so a regression that prints the wrong format (for example JSON in `--anchor` mode or a single hash in map mode) would still pass the suite.

`expected_members: []` is described here as the only legitimate case for a unit-like struct, but D2 later explicitly allows empty `expected_members` for `UseCase`/`Interactor`/`Factory`, `Dto`/`Command`/`Query`, `Typestate`, and `SecondaryAdapter` when they have no public fields. Those rules conflict, so the empty-vec semantics for `expected_members` are not internally consistent.

D4’s `FreeFunction` schema only declares `expected_params` and `expected_returns`, but the same section later treats `async`/sync as part of signature matching. Because the catalog-side declaration has no field for that dimension, the plan cannot represent or validate async mismatches as written.

D3 broadens reverse checking to every workspace-origin trait impl, but that conflicts with the earlier secondary-adapter scope decision that excludes infrastructure-internal traits. As written, traits like `GitRepository`/`GhClient` would become contract violations even though they are intentionally out of scope, so this check will produce false positives on the existing workspace.

D4 models free functions as `functions: HashMap<String, FunctionNode>` keyed only by the short name, but it never records a module path or any other namespace. That means two public functions with the same name in different modules cannot be represented safely, and the later 'same module' reverse-extra rule has no identifier to evaluate.

`expected_members` semantics are inconsistent: this section says empty `Vec` is valid only for unit-like structs, but the `UseCase`/`Interactor`/`Factory` and `SecondaryAdapter` rules immediately below also allow empty `Vec` for structs that simply have no public fields. Those two rules cannot both hold without redefining the empty-`Vec` rule.

The new empty-`Vec` criterion says empty `Vec` is allowed whenever a struct has zero public fields, but the same ADR still requires tuple structs to be declared by positional indices (for example `struct Email(String)` must use `"0"`). That makes tuple structs with private fields both eligible for omission and required to be listed, so the `expected_members` rule is internally inconsistent.

D3’s reverse-check inclusion rule is too broad: it treats every trait whose `origin_crate` is in the workspace as in-scope, which would also pull in infrastructure-local traits such as `GitRepository` and `GhClient`. That conflicts with the earlier SecondaryAdapter track decision that those internal traits are out of scope, so `SystemGitRepo` / `SystemGhClient` would become false positives.

The zero-member exception for `expected_members` is too broad to be implementable as written. The plan allows `expected_members: []` for tuple structs with all fields private, but the current TypeGraph export path still emits tuple fields, so those structs do not actually appear member-less in the data the evaluator reads.

D4 says a FreeFunction signature match includes async-ness, and the TypeGraph/baseline entries both carry `is_async`, but the catalog declaration shape only has `expected_params` and `expected_returns`. That leaves no catalog-side value to compare against `is_async`, so the proposed 3-way bundle is internally incomplete and the stated forward-miss rule cannot be implemented as written.

This paragraph says adding `TypeBaselineEntry::trait_impls` resolves the above missing baseline state, but the only missing baseline item listed immediately above is the `functions` map for D4. As written, D3's trait-impl baseline addition is presented as resolving D4's missing functions snapshot, which contradicts the surrounding bundle explanation.

§3 says `declares_application_service` needs no TypeGraph extension because `TypeNode::trait_impls` already exists, but the ADR later makes `TraitImplEntry::origin_crate` a required D3 TypeGraph schema extension and bundles it with baseline `trait_impls`. This contradiction undermines the 3-part schema bundle invariant and could lead the implementation plan to omit the required TypeGraph update.

The D3 consequence states type-designer declaration omissions are detected as `reverse extra` and therefore Red, but §S explicitly maps `reverse extra` to Yellow for `modify` and `delete` actions. This leaves an action-neutral `reverse extra -> Red` assertion in the ADR, contradicting the action-specific signal table.

D4’s reverse-extra rule is defined as detecting undeclared free functions in the same module, but the proposed TypeGraph/baseline bundle only stores `params`, `returns`, and `is_async` for each function and never defines the `functions` map key as module-qualified or stores a `module_path`. Without module identity, the evaluator cannot distinguish same-named functions in different modules or enumerate extras only within the declared module, so the D4 signal rule is not executable while the ADR claims the three-schema bundle is complete.

D4 says same-named free functions in different modules are distinguished by `name + module_path`, but the proposed `TypeGraph::functions` / baseline schema is still `HashMap<String, FunctionNode>` keyed by the short name. With that shape, two `my_func` definitions in different modules collide before `module_path` can disambiguate them, so the plan cannot satisfy its own reverse/forward matching rule for module-scoped free functions.

D4's baseline bundle is internally contradictory: this paragraph says `TypeBaseline` will add `functions: HashMap<String, FunctionBaselineEntry>`, but the same section already requires a tuple key `HashMap<(String, Option<String>), FunctionBaselineEntry>` and D4 later relies on that tuple key to avoid same-name function collisions across modules. This leaves the planned baseline schema ambiguous for implementers.

The D4 signal summary still defines `reverse extra` by function name alone, while the D4 decision later defines the matching axis as `(name, module_path)` and explicitly says same-name functions in different modules must not be treated as reverse extras. These two rules conflict and would produce different signal behavior for common free-function names such as `new`, `build`, or `parse` across modules.

D4's catalog-side `FreeFunction` shape only adds `expected_params`, `expected_returns`, and `expected_is_async`, but the same decision later defines matching and reverse-extra evaluation by `(name, module_path)`. Since the catalogue declaration has no field that supplies the declared `module_path`, the type-designer cannot distinguish same-named functions in different modules or scope reverse-extra checks to the declared module. That makes the claimed catalog/TypeGraph/baseline bundle incomplete as written.

The ADR gives incompatible D4 reverse-extra definitions. Here it says an implementation function is extra when its `(name, module_path)` pair is undeclared, which implies a same-named function in another module is extra if that pair was not declared. Later D4 narrows reverse extra to undeclared names within the same `module_path` and says different-module same-name functions are not extras. Those rules lead to different evaluator behavior, so the plan needs one canonical reverse-check scope.

D4 specifies `TypeBaseline::functions` as `HashMap<(String, Option<String>), FunctionBaselineEntry>` for a JSON baseline, but serde JSON map keys must serialize as strings. Without defining a string key/newtype/custom serialization, baseline capture for this schema cannot be written/read, so the D4 three-way bundle is not executable as stated.

A9 says the adopted D3 approach uses “catalogue declaration based” range selection, but D3’s actual decision is to reverse-check all workspace-origin traits via `TraitImplEntry::origin_crate`. That contradiction can steer implementation back to catalogue-only scoping, which would miss undeclared workspace traits that D3 is meant to detect.

GO-03 says this track resolves Contract Map limitation §L2 as a whole, but §L2 explicitly includes field-reference and mutation-only/declaration-only cases, while OS-03 puts field edges out of scope and the in-scope/acceptance items only cover the FreeFunction part of §L2. This makes the plan overclaim the scope: implementers can satisfy the scoped work while the stated goal remains false. Narrow GO-03 to the specific §L2 subcase being handled, or bring the remaining §L2 remediation into scope with acceptance criteria.

AC-09 requires FreeFunction parameter/return references to be rendered as “method call edge”, but the ADR being implemented defines FreeFunction integration only as edges from the FreeFunction node to expected_params/expected_returns; “method call edge” is specifically for `A.method()` references. This mislabels the new edge semantics and can drive an implementation to emit method-shaped edges for non-method free functions instead of the D4 FreeFunction edges.

GO-03 claims this track resolves ADR 2026-04-17-1528 §L2 as a whole, but §L2 also covers field-reference and mutation-only declaration cases while OS-03 explicitly keeps field edges out of scope. This makes the goal broader than the scoped work can satisfy; narrow GO-03 to the FreeFunction-argument part of §L2, or move the remaining §L2 cases into scope with acceptance criteria.

CN-03 says an empty expected_members Vec is a design error except for marker structs, but the ADR permits empty expected_members whenever TypeGraph exposes zero public members, including unit-like structs, all-private-field structs, and UseCase/Interactor/Factory structs that receive dependencies via constructors. As written, this constraint would overconstrain valid catalogue entries and contradicts the ADR’s D2 semantics.

GO-03 says the track will also resolve a FreeFunction error-type edge, but the scoped spec and plan only define FreeFunction `expected_params` / `expected_returns` edges. There is no schema field or task that can represent or implement an error edge, so this goal is broader than the planned work can satisfy.

IN-10 requires workspace-origin filtering for both Interactor and SecondaryAdapter reverse checks, but the implementation plan only schedules the Interactor evaluator in T008. `evaluate_secondary_adapter` is only made action-aware in T001 and never gets the `origin_crate` filter, so the SecondaryAdapter half of the scoped requirement is uncovered.

FunctionNode, FunctionBaselineEntry, and TraitImplBaselineEntry are described as newly added types for D3/D4, but these catalogue entries omit an `action` field, and the rendered domain-types.md shows their action as `—` with Yellow signals. That contradicts the plan tasks that add these types (T004/T005) and leaves their action-aware signal semantics undefined; they should be catalogued with an explicit `add` action or the narrative should stop describing them as new additions.

TypeCatalogueEntry is marked `action: "reference"`, but its description and informal ground both say it gains/will carry `expected_members` and `declares_application_service`. This conflicts with the action and with the implementation plan, which places those schema additions on TypeDefinitionKind/codec work; as written it either under-catalogues a required TypeCatalogueEntry modification or points the implementer at the wrong domain type.

The rendered `domain-types.md` is still not uniformly blue: `TypeDefinitionKind` and the new DTO rows (`FunctionNode`, `FunctionBaselineEntry`, `TraitImplBaselineEntry`) render as yellow, which contradicts `spec.md`'s 42-blue / 0-yellow summary and the briefing's claim that the layer signals are all blue.

GO-03 overstates scope by saying the track will resolve a `FreeFunction` error-type edge, but the scoped design (`IN-11`, `AC-09`, `T009`) only defines `expected_params` / `expected_returns` type edges. There is no corresponding schema field or task for an error edge, so the goal cannot be satisfied as written.

`spec.md` says Stage 1 has `0 yellow / 0 red`, but `domain-types.md` still renders yellow rows for `TypeDefinitionKind` and the new `FunctionNode` / `FunctionBaselineEntry` / `TraitImplBaselineEntry` entries. The regenerated type view is therefore stale relative to the refreshed plan artifacts.

`domain-types.md` renders the entire `Signal` column as `—`, which contradicts the dual-axis model in the briefing and the generated `domain-type-signals.json`: `TypeDefinitionKind` is actually yellow because `FreeFunction` is still missing, and the new `FunctionNode` / `FunctionBaselineEntry` / `TraitImplBaselineEntry` entries are also yellow rather than blank.

This says the TypeGraph/baseline schema work will be completed "simultaneously" across S3/S4, but S4 is explicitly blocked until S3 finishes. The dependency order contradicts the stated core invariant and makes the parallelization claim inaccurate.

GO-03 claims the track will also draw a distinct "FreeFunction error-type edge", but T009 and AC-09 only specify parameter and return-type edges. There is no matching task or acceptance criterion for a separate error-type edge, so the goal statement overstates the scoped deliverable.

`typestate` is missing from the cross-kind rejection pass, so payloads like `{ "kind": "typestate", "expected_methods": [...] }` or `{ "kind": "typestate", "expected_variants": [...] }` will be accepted and those stale keys silently dropped by serde. That leaves the new codec partially validating the new schema instead of rejecting illegal field combinations at the boundary.

`FreeFunction` decode/encode validates param/member L1 identifiers, but `expected_returns` is copied through verbatim with no `::` check. A fully-qualified return like `std::io::Error` will round-trip instead of being rejected, which breaks the same L1 rule enforced for method returns and lets invalid catalogue data through.

`MemberDeclarationDto` is the only nested leaf DTO here without `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]`, so typos or stale keys inside `expected_members` entries are silently discarded instead of failing decode. That creates an inconsistent, lossy input path compared with the other DTOs in this codec.

The new Phase 1.5 validation never rejects `expected_members` on kinds that do not define it (`enum`, `error_type`, `secondary_port`, `application_service`, `free_function`). Because `TypeCatalogueEntryDto` flattens the kind payload and does not deny unknown fields, those extra members are silently discarded by serde and malformed catalogues are accepted.

`typestate` is no longer covered by the stale-field guard for `expected_methods` / `expected_variants`. The later `implements` check is the only typestate-specific filter, so a typestate entry carrying those foreign fields will still decode successfully and the data will be dropped.

`FreeFunction` now validates `expected_params`, but `expected_returns` is never L1-checked for `::` on either decode or encode. That lets qualified return paths such as `std::io::Error` slip through even though the file-level contract says returns strings containing `::` must be rejected.

The new non-empty `expected_variants` rule for `enum`/`error_type` is enforced only during `decode()`. `encode()` still serializes `Enum { expected_variants: vec![] }` and `ErrorType { expected_variants: vec![] }` unchanged, so a public caller can emit JSON that this codec rejects on the next read.

Phase 1.5 still only rejects a subset of stale fields. New fields introduced in this patch (`module_path`, `expected_params`, `expected_returns`, `expected_is_async`, `declares_application_service`) are not blocked on unrelated kinds, so payloads such as `{"kind":"enum","module_path":"x"}` or `{"kind":"typestate","expected_params":[...]}` decode successfully with those fields silently dropped.

`MemberDeclarationDto` is a tagged enum without `deny_unknown_fields`, unlike the other nested DTOs. Extra keys inside `expected_members[]` entries are therefore accepted and discarded instead of failing the decode, which breaks strict schema validation for the new member codec.

`decode_member_list` / `encode_member_list` only validate `Field.ty` for `::`; they never reject `::` in the member identifier itself. As a result, `expected_members` can carry impossible names like `foo::Bar` in both `Variant` and `Field.name`, and the codec will accept/emit them instead of enforcing the new L1 rule.

`build_trait_origins` collapses all impls by the trait short name and keeps the first entry seen. If a crate has two traits with the same short name (for example `std::fmt::Display` and a local `foo::Display`), `TraitImplEntry::origin_crate` becomes whichever one happened to be visited first in `krate.index.values()`. Because that iteration order comes from a `HashMap`, the result is nondeterministic and can mislabel trait origins, which breaks the downstream filtering that depends on this field.

The new rustdoc extraction path in `schema_export.rs` has no direct test coverage for the added behavior: `functions()` population and `trait_origins` derivation. The existing integration tests still only assert non-empty types/traits/impls and parseable JSON, so regressions in `extract_module_path` or `build_trait_origins` would not be caught.

`build_trait_origins` collapses all trait definitions to a `short_name -> crate` map, so a crate that defines its own trait with the same short name as an external trait will mislabel the external impls. For example, if the crate has a local `Display` and also implements `std::fmt::Display`, every `Display` impl is assigned `origin_crate = crate_name`, which breaks the new workspace/external filtering for common name collisions.

`build_trait_origins` says the alphabetically-first full path should win when two external traits share a short name, but the `and_modify` branch overwrites an earlier external entry with any later external entry. That makes `origin_crate` wrong for external/external collisions (the last path wins instead of the first).

`build_trait_origins` deduplicates candidates by `trait_path.path`, but rustdoc's `Path.path` is the use-site path, not the definition path, and can be just `Display`/an alias. If a local trait and an external trait with the same short/use path are implemented in different modules, the `contains_key` check makes whichever impl happens to appear first in `krate.index.values()` win before the later local-precedence collapse runs, so `origin_crate` can be nondeterministic or wrong. Key the deterministic map from `krate.paths[trait_id].path` (or include the trait id) and only collapse to the short-name lookup key afterward.
